items.q: What are the 4 Cs of food hygiene?
items.correct: Cleaning, Cooking, Chilling, and avoiding Cross-contamination.
items.why: Students guess at the Cs. They specifically address the four food-safety risks: dirty surfaces, undercooked food, warm storage, and pathogens transferring between foods (raw to ready-to-eat).

items.q: On a traffic-light label, what does a red light indicate?
items.correct: High amounts of that nutrient per 100g - eat less often.
items.misconception: The food is dangerous and should never be eaten.
items.why: Students treat red as 'forbidden'. Traffic lights are a guidance system - red = high, eat less often or in small portions. Many foods (cheese, olive oil) are red on certain nutrients but fine in moderation.

items.q: Which macronutrient provides the most energy per gram?
items.why: Students assume carbs (the 'energy food') provide most energy per gram. Carbs and protein = 4 kcal/g; FAT = 9 kcal/g (most energy-dense). Vitamins provide no energy at all.

items.correct: No - free-range concerns outdoor access for the animals; organic is a wider standard covering feed, land and restricted chemical use.
items.misconception: Yes - free-range and organic are interchangeable labels.
items.why: Both labels signal higher welfare/standards, so students merge them. Free-range eggs come from hens with daytime outdoor access, but the hens' feed need not be organic. Organic certification regulates feed, land management and medicines as well as welfare.

items.q: Do genetically modified foods contain genes while ordinary foods do not?
items.correct: No - all plant and animal foods naturally contain genes; GM foods have had their genetic material deliberately altered.
items.misconception: Only GM foods contain genes; normal food is gene-free.
items.random_wrong: GM foods are grown without soil.
items.why: Survey-famous misconception. DNA is in every cell of every plant and animal we eat. The GM difference is that scientists have changed or inserted specific genes (e.g. for pest resistance) - not that genes are present at all.

items.q: How many portions of fruit and veg a day are recommended?
items.why: Correct answer: 5. “10” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Eatwell Guide is from which country?
items.why: Correct answer: UK. “USA” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Recommended portions of oily fish per week?
items.why: Exact answer: 1 portion. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
